case_name: Automated Passenger Services Permitting Scheme Consultation

entities.name: Secretary of State for Transport
entities.description: The appropriate national authority for granting APS permits for services resembling taxis/PHVs in England and bus/coach services anywhere in Great Britain. May delegate functions to DVSA.

entities.name: Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency
entities.description: The body intended to grant APS permit applications on behalf of the Secretary of State for Transport. Also conducts internal reviews of decisions.

entities.name: Centre for Connected and Autonomous Vehicles
entities.description: The unit within the Department for Transport that is leading the consultation on the APS permitting scheme.

entities.name: Consenting Authority
entities.description: Local licensing authority (for taxi/PHV-like services) or franchising body (for bus-like services) whose consent is required before a permit can be issued. Must respond within 6 weeks; silence gives automatic consent.

entities.name: Traffic Authority
entities.description: Authority defined by the Road Traffic Regulation Act 1984 s.121A that must be consulted before a permit is granted if likely to be substantially affected.

entities.name: Emergency Services
entities.description: Emergency services likely to be substantially affected by a permit, which must be consulted before grant.

entities.name: APS Permit Holder
entities.description: Organisation granted an APS permit to operate an automated passenger service. Subject to conditions, reporting obligations, and potential variation/suspension/withdrawal.

entities.name: APS Applicant
entities.description: Organisation applying for an APS permit. Must provide information on service scope, operational competence, safeguarding, insurance, and engagement with stakeholders.

entities.name: Older and Disabled Passengers
entities.description: Passenger group whose needs must be considered in permit decisions and reported on by permit holders. Around 1 in 4 people in the UK report having a disability.

stakeholders.name: Centre for Connected and Autonomous Vehicles

assumptions.statement: The APS permitting scheme will provide a clear legal route for deploying commercial passenger services with no human driver, giving operators certainty to enter the GB market.
Without the APS permitting scheme, automated passenger services 'may either be banned or entirely unregulated' (Law Commissions).
The APS permitting scheme will sit alongside existing taxi/PHV/PSV licensing routes, not replace them.
Consent from local licensing authorities or franchising bodies is critical for clarity and successful deployment.
The Secretary of State must consider whether granting a permit is likely to improve understanding of how APS should be designed for older and disabled passengers.
Permit holders must publish reports about their service, particularly steps taken to meet needs of older/disabled passengers and safeguard passengers generally.
The application and renewal processes should be flexible rather than fixed by legislation, allowing revision over time.
Civil sanctions will not be implemented for pilots; variation/suspension/withdrawal will be the enforcement means initially.
A formal appeals system in court or tribunal is not proposed at this stage; internal DVSA review is provided instead.
Fees will not be charged initially; the intention is to introduce fees for full cost recovery after full implementation of the AV Act.

constraints.statement: The APS permitting scheme is introduced through the Automated Vehicles (AV) Act 2024 and secondary legislation; the consultation does not seek views on the Act itself.
Permits may only be issued with consent of the licensing authority for each place where a taxi/PHV-like service is provided, or franchising authority for bus-like services in franchising areas.
The Secretary of State must consult traffic authorities and emergency services likely to be substantially affected before granting a permit.
Permits must specify the area, vehicles (or description), and validity period; conditions may limit service or impose obligations.
The maximum permit validity period is proposed as 5 years; initial pilot permits expected to be 12-18 months.

Permits may be varied, suspended, or withdrawn on grounds including breach of condition, serious/repeated traffic infractions, misrepresentation, material change of circumstances, serious safety concerns, or unroadworthiness.
Information shared under permit conditions is protected; unauthorised disclosure is a criminal offence carrying an unlimited fine.
The Public Sector Equality Duty applies to the Secretary of State, consenting authorities, and service providers carrying out a role for a public authority.
Consenting authorities have 6 weeks to respond to a consent request; no response within that period results in automatic consent.
Renewal applications must be made within a window beginning 6 months before expiry and ending 2 months before expiry; if applied within window, permit continues until decision or 5-year maximum.
Internal review requests must be made within 28 days of the decision; DVSA must confirm receipt within 14 days and provide an anticipated outcome date.
In urgent cases, the Secretary of State may suspend or temporarily vary a permit first, then invite representations.
The APS permitting scheme does not apply in Northern Ireland.
This consultation covers services resembling taxis/PHVs in England and bus/coach services anywhere in Great Britain.
The APS permitting scheme is not a bypass to avoid licensing routes for normal taxis, PHVs, or PSVs.
Reports must be decision support, not deterministic forecast.
Agents must be archetypes only and must not process real-person PII.
Agents must not automatically send blockchain transactions.
Agents must not automatically change stakeholder weights.
